B.unlocks: Progressive tensioning, slip-proof fixtures, and mechanical memory of tightening.
B.justification: The ratchet stores state (tooth index) and prevents backdrive, acting like a cheap one-way linear motor.

Pulley mockup: route as a low-friction guide for cable path prototyping

A: Give 8 unconventional uses for a foam earplug focusing on damping, sealing, and compliance.

B.rule: Treat it as a viscoelastic, slow-recovery microcellular material: compressible seal + vibration damper.
B.unlocks: Leak sealing, resonance suppression, and gentle fixturing in tight tolerances.
B.justification: Foam cell structure dissipates energy and conforms to surfaces, forming seals without high clamping force.

Cable pass-through gasket: compress around wires to seal a hole
Impact buffer: place at contact points to reduce shock in a case
Friction clutch pad: add to a knob to increase grip and damping
Temporary acoustic baffle: tune a small enclosure by partially sealing ports
Gentle clamp insert: add to jaws to hold fragile parts without marring

A: Give 8 unconventional uses for a glass bottle focusing on resonance, pressure, and optics.

A: Give 8 unconventional uses for a towel focusing on shear, friction, and boundary layers.

B.unlocks: Slip control, airflow/drag experiments, and controlled evaporation for cooling.

Evaporative cooler sleeve: wrap a bottle and wet for passive cooling

One-way winch demo: loop and pull to preload a spring without slip

A: Give 8 unusual uses for sticky notes focusing on prototyping and human factors.

B.rule: Treat each note as a movable state token: position encodes state, color encodes attributes, and movement frequency encodes transition cost.
B.unlocks: Rapid workflow simulation, bottleneck discovery, and low-cost usability testing.
B.justification: Token accumulation exposes delays; physical movement externalizes cognitive friction and makes it measurable.

C: Throughput map: move one token per step to reveal bottlenecks
Error tracing: color tokens for failure modes and see where they pile up
State tokens: move notes between columns to simulate workflow without building UI
Transition-cost timing: measure hesitation before moving tokens
Capacity limit: cap a column to N notes to prevent overload
Path comparison: two boards, same tokens, compare backtracking
Attention probe: cover part of a checklist and see what gets missed
Workload heat proxy: stack height indicates where work accumulates

A: Give 8 unconventional uses for a ruler focusing on metrology and error estimation.

B.rule: Treat it as a rigid straight edge and fixed-length transfer standard for geometric and optical reference (shadows, reflections, alignment).
B.unlocks: Shadow-based length transfer, optical alignment, and angle reference beyond direct measurement.
B.justification: A rigid opaque edge produces repeatable shadows and reflections; fixed length enables ratio and proportion transfer without calibration.

C: Shadow length transfer: use sun angle and ruler shadow to estimate height of a pole
Optical straightedge: sight along edge to check alignment of distant features
Reflection alignment: use ruler edge in a mirror to set perpendicular
Fixed-ratio transfer: use ruler length to step off proportional distances on a wall
Sight-line jig: align two marks by viewing along the edge
Parallax reference: hold at fixed distance to estimate depth in a scene
Edge-of-field marker: place at frame edge for consistent photo framing
Photogrammetry scale: include ruler in shot for later 3D scale recovery

A: Give 8 unusual uses for a notebook focusing on experimental design and logging.

B.rule: Treat it as an external memory buffer that enables reproducibility: versioning, protocols, and decision logs reduce confabulation.
B.unlocks: A/B tests, bias control, and traceable iteration in creative or technical workflows.
B.justification: Humans forget and reconstruct; persistent logs create comparable records and allow controlled evaluation.

C: Pre-registered rubric pages to score outputs consistently
Idea version control: commit-style entries with rationale/outcomes
Breakage log: record failure conditions and photos to spot patterns
Calibration ledger: record instrument offsets and drift over time
Latency diary: timestamp steps to find hidden bottlenecks
Decision trace map: log assumptions then mark which failed
User-test script: standardized prompts for comparable sessions
Experiment cards: one page per trial with fixed fields to reduce bias

A: Give 8 unconventional uses for a paper napkin focusing on capillarity and surface interactions.

B.rule: Treat it as a thin porous capillary membrane: fiber density and folding set wicking speed, filtration, and evaporation area.
B.unlocks: Wicking diagnostics, passive filtering, and controlled evaporation patterns beyond wiping.
B.justification: Capillary pressure drives liquid uptake; folding changes thickness and flow resistance; the fiber network traps particulates.

C: Wick bridge: move water from a puddle edge into a cup
Leak direction tracer: touch seam; wicking path shows seep source
Evaporation map: wet squares and compare dry time vs fold thickness
DIY particulate pre-filter for dirty rinse water (non-potable)
Capillary rise demo: measure height vs time with colored water
Ink bleed test: compare marker spread across different napkin folds
Humidity micro-chamber: damp napkin in a box to stabilize humidity
Oil/water discrimination: show oil beads while water wicks quickly

A: Give 8 unusual uses for a clothespin focusing on force limiting and mechanical triggering.
D: Clip a photo to a line
Clamp a note to a board

B.rule: Treat it as a spring clamp with a binary jaw-state event: jaw closure/opening is a detectable threshold and clamp force is roughly repeatable.
B.unlocks: Force-limited fixturing and simple mechanical ‘switches’ beyond clipping.
B.justification: Spring preload yields near-constant clamp force; the jaw motion provides a clear mechanical transition for triggering.

C: Touch sensor: foil on jaws closes a circuit when object is gripped
Pull-threshold indicator: string slips when tension exceeds clamp friction
Release trigger: clip holds a flag that pops free when bumped
Delicate-part clamp: hold foam parts without crushing from over-tightening
Vibration telltale: clip on a thin rod; chatter indicates resonance
Edge fixture: hold a stencil edge without adhesive residue
Quick hinge: use the pin axis as a pivot in cardboard prototypes
Strain relief: clamp a cable to desk edge to absorb accidental tugs

A: Give 8 unconventional uses for a stack of sticky labels focusing on tracking and error-proofing.

B.rule: Treat each label as a portable metadata packet: placement encodes state, ownership, and priority in physical space.
B.unlocks: Visual process control, audit trails, and mistake-proofing without software.
B.justification: Externalizing status reduces memory load; standardized tag placement makes workflow state instantly legible.

C: State tags: move label location to show draft/review/final status
Defect tokens: label a flaw with an ID and log later
Cable-end pairing: match codes on both ends for quick tracing
Batch integrity seals: label sets so parts don’t mix across experiments
Timebox flags: label an item with “stop at 15:00” for discipline
Ownership boundary: add return-by dates on shared tools
Blind evaluation: encode condition IDs without revealing meanings
Handoff log: each transfer gets a new label layer as a custody trail

A: Give 8 unusual uses for a piece of foam (packaging foam) focusing on damping and fixturing.
D: Protect an item in a box

B.rule: Treat it as a viscoelastic, compressible cellular solid: compression sets stiffness, and internal friction dissipates vibration energy.
B.unlocks: Vibration isolation, preload fixturing, and impact buffering with tunable stiffness.
B.justification: Cellular structure buckles and dissipates energy; varying thickness and compression changes the force–displacement curve.

C: Vibration isolator pad under a noisy desk device
Preload spacer: compress to hold a part snugly without screws
Shock witness: compare dent depth to estimate impact severity
Soft jaw insert: protect surfaces in a clamp/vise
Resonance damper wedge: stop a panel buzz by adding frictional loss
Drop-test catcher: consistent landing pad for prototype comparisons
Cable strain boot: foam sleeve to reduce bend stress near connectors
Alignment cradle: cut a V-groove to hold cylinders during cutting

A: Give 8 unconventional uses for a transparent plastic sheet (report cover) focusing on optics and alignment.

B.rule: Treat it as a transparent optical overlay and low-friction laminate: it preserves line-of-sight for alignment while providing a controlled sliding interface.
B.unlocks: Overlay-based registration, glare experiments, and reusable masking surfaces beyond protection.
B.justification: Transparency enables direct visual registration; smooth plastic reduces friction and protects surfaces during repeated adjustments.

C: Overlay alignment: compare two prints by sliding until features coincide
Glare mapping: tilt and record angles where specular hotspots appear
Reusable stencil carrier: tape a paper stencil underneath for durability
Low-friction slider stage: move a sample smoothly for inspection photos
Dust shield window: cover a display while still reading it
Angle reference: draw lines on sheet and use as a movable overlay protractor
Smear detector: wipe pattern shows contamination films on the sheet
Spill barrier: protect desk during small fluid experiments (non-hazardous)

A: Give 8 unconventional uses for a hair dryer (cold or low-heat setting) focusing on airflow and convection.

B.rule: Treat it as a directed, tunable airflow source with variable velocity and temperature: a portable wind tunnel and convection driver.
B.unlocks: Flow visualization, forced convection experiments, and particle or vapor transport beyond drying.
B.justification: Controlled airflow enables repeatable boundary-layer and convection effects; cold setting avoids thermal damage to materials.

C: Flow tracer: direct stream at smoke or powder to visualize room airflow
Convection benchmark: compare cooling of two warm objects with and without directed flow
Dust evacuator: blow debris out of narrow channels or crevices in prototypes
Mold-release aid: low heat to soften wax or shrink-wrap for clean demolding
Condensation clearer: cold air stream to defog a lens or window locally
Particle separator: blow light chaff off a surface while heavy parts stay put
Vapor dilutor: disperse a localized odor or vapor pocket with directed flow
Boundary-layer demo: feel drag difference holding a plate flat vs edge-on to the stream

A: Give 8 unusual uses for a kitchen funnel focusing on flow convergence and controlled dispensing.
D: Add flour to a narrow neck

B.rule: Treat it as a flow concentrator and passive flow-rate limiter: cross-section and angle set throughput and prevent splashing.
B.unlocks: Controlled dispensing, separation by flow path, and single-point collection from a wide catchment.
B.justification: Converging geometry reduces velocity spread and directs mass to one exit; tilt and orifice size control flow rate.

C: Drip collector: place under a leaky joint to route drips into a container for measurement
Powder dispenser: tap funnel to meter granular flow into small vials
Two-phase separator: pour mixture so liquid exits first, then tip to dump solids
Splash guard: pour through funnel into narrow opening without spill
Flow-rate comparator: time equal volumes through different funnel sizes
Seed sorter: small seeds fall through, larger ones collect for hand removal
Funnel as mold: pour plaster or wax and remove when set for a cone shape
Catch basin: hold under a dripping tap to measure drip rate over time

A: Give 8 unconventional uses for a wine cork (natural or synthetic) in a lab or workshop focusing on compressibility and sealing.

B.rule: Treat it as a compressible, low-friction seal and vibration damper: insertion force and recovery set sealing pressure and acoustic absorption.
B.unlocks: Reversible sealing, impact absorption, and compliant fixturing beyond bottle closure.
B.justification: Cork compresses under load and recovers; friction holds it in place. Cellular structure absorbs sound and impact.

C: Test-tube stopper: seal a tube for shaking or inversion without a dedicated cap
Vibration pad: mount a small motor or pump on cork feet to reduce transmitted buzz
Non-marring clamp insert: press part against cork to hold without scratching
Temporary valve: insert cork into a tube and pull partway to tune airflow
Impact absorber: drop a fragile item onto a cork mat and compare bounce
Acoustic damper: wedge cork between two panels to reduce resonance
Leveling shim: compress cork under a wobbly leg for a stable fit
Seal tester: press cork into a neck and submerge to check for bubbles
